title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
#Getting started
There is a need to check if we have installed the relevant packages.

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes, 
               tidyverse)

Import and read the data set, which is the Exam_data.csv file

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

Making different plots using ggplot.
First plot is to compare the English and Maths scores for Primary 3.

#ggplot(data=exam_data,
#       aes(x=MATHS,
#           y=ENGLISH))
#+ geom_point() 
#+ geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
#              vjust= -.5)
#+ coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
#                  ylim=c(0,100))

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Getting started
There is a need to check if we have installed the relevant packages.

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes, 
               tidyverse)

Import and read the data set, which is the Exam_data.csv file

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")



Making different plots using ggplot.
First plot is to compare the English and Maths scores for Primary 3.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS,
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
              vjust= -.5)+ 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))+ 
  ggtitle("English scores versus Math scores for Primary 3")








Second plot is to compare the Science and Maths scores for Primary 3.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS,
           y=SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
              vjust= -.5)+ 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))+ 
  ggtitle("English scores versus Math scores for Primary 3")








I am going to plot the same type of graph to compare race and Maths scores.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=RACE,
           y=SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
              vjust= -.5)+ 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))+ 
  ggtitle("English scores versus Math scores for Primary 3")








This plot appears strange as RACE is a categorical data type. Hence, the few values of RACE are compressed to the far left, making it difficult to interpret.
A histogram or bar chart would be a better choice to plot a categorical and numerical into one plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=SCIENCE))+
  geom_histogram(bins=25,
                 boundary=100,
                 color="grey25",
                 fill="grey90")+
  ggtitle("Distribution of Science scores")+
  theme_economist()








This plot is much easier to interpret. It is important to take note of data types when deciding what plots,charts and graphs to use.
I will make another plot to see the count of students by race. However, the code below will give an error as geom_histogram() is for continuous numeric data, but RACE is a categorical/discrete variable.That is why ggplot says:stat_bin() requires a continuous x aesthetic.

#ggplot(data=exam_data,
#       aes(x=RACE))+
#  geom_histogram(bins=25,
#                 boundary=100,
#                 color="grey25",
#                fill="grey90")+
#  ggtitle("Distribution of Science scores")+
#  theme_economist()

Instead, we can use geom_bar().

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(color = "grey25",
           fill = "grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Race") +
  theme_economist()










Using ggrepel:

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")










Working with ggtheme package:

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()









ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()









ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")










Combining more than one graph into a single display:
Here we will assign the individual plots to a variable.

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
          aes(x=MATHS))+
      geom_histogram(bins=20,
                     boundary=100,
                     color="grey25",
                     fill="grey90")+
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100))+
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths Score")


p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
             aes(x=ENGLISH))+
  geom_histogram(bins=20,
                 boundary=100,
                 color="blue2",
                 fill="white")+
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100))+
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")


p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

Now, combine the 2 plots into one.

p1 + p2








Lets combine 3 into one.

p1+p2+p3








In the above plot, some portions of the title are cut out. So, we increase the width and height of the area to plot.

p1 + p2 + p3








There are other ways to display the charts into 1.
“/” operator to stack two ggplot2 graphs, “|” operator to place the plots beside each other, “()” operator the define the sequence of the plotting.

(p1 / p2) | p3








tagging/numbering:

((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')








Inserting the plot at specific locations/areas:

p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)









patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()










Alternatives

Label only selected points instead of every point


nrow(subset(exam_data, MATHS > 80 | ENGLISH > 80))

[1] 117

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_label_repel(
    data = subset(exam_data, MATHS > 80 | ENGLISH > 80),
    aes(label = ID),
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for P3")









Use geom_text_repel()


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = ID),
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 3
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for P3")









Map color to a grouping variable in the labelled scatterplot


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH, colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5, se = FALSE) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = subset(exam_data, MATHS > 85 | ENGLISH > 85),
    aes(label = ID),
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English V Maths scores by Gender")









Using a different theme instead of ggthemes. Use theme_wsj()


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 20,
    boundary = 100,
    color = "grey25",
    fill = "grey90"
  ) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_fivethirtyeight()









Use theme()


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 20,
    boundary = 100,
    color = "grey25",
    fill = "grey90"
  ) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11)
  )









Combine 2 plots with wrap_plots()


wrap_plots(p1, p2, ncol = 2)









Adding overall title and subtitle


(p1 / p2) | p3 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Exam Performance",
    subtitle = "Distribution of Maths & English scores with relationship plot",
    tag_levels = "A"
  )

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, 
               patchwork, DT, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
using ggiraph package

The plotThe code











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE, 
    binwidth = 1, 
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, 
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Style 1

The plotThe code











tooltip_css <- "background-color:white; #<<
font-style:bold; color:black;" #<<

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = ID),                   
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(    #<<
    opts_tooltip(    #<<
      css = tooltip_css)) #<<
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
Hands-on Exercise 3
Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Displaying median instead of mean

The plotThe code











tooltip <- function(y, ymin, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  median_val <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Median maths scores:", median_val)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE)) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS,
        tooltip = after_stat(
          tooltip(y, ymin, ymax)
        )),
    fun = median,                  
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = "lightblue"
  ) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = median_hilow,       
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    size = 0.2
  )

girafe(
  ggobj = gg_point,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
section: Displaying standard deviation instead of mean
text: Displaying standard deviation instead of mean

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Displaying standard deviation

The plotThe code











mean_sd <- function(x) {
  m <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  s <- sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  data.frame(
    y = m,
    ymin = m - s,
    ymax = m + s
  )
}

tooltip <- function(y, ymin, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean_val <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sd_val <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean_val, "\nSD:", sd_val)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE)) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS,
        tooltip = after_stat(
          tooltip(y, ymin, ymax)
        )),
    fun.data = mean_sd,                 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = "lightblue"
  ) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_sd,                 
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    size = 0.2
  )

girafe(
  ggobj = gg_point,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8 * 0.618
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
adding a visual element

The plotThe code











plot_ly(data = exam_data, 
        x = ~ENGLISH, 
        y = ~MATHS, 
        color = ~RACE)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
section: Creating an interactive scatterplot, ggplotly method
text:
Creating an interactive scatterplot, ggplotly method

The plotThe code











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
ggplotly(p)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Coordinated linked plot, using plotly

The plotThe code











d <- highlight_key(exam_data)
p1 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

p2 <- ggplot(data=d, 
            aes(x = MATHS,
                y = SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))
subplot(ggplotly(p1),
        ggplotly(p2))

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Linked Brushing

The plotThe code





















d <- highlight_key(exam_data) 
p <- ggplot(d, 
            aes(ENGLISH, 
                MATHS)) + 
  geom_point(size=1) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))

gg <- highlight(ggplotly(p),        
                "plotly_selected")  

crosstalk::bscols(gg,               
                  DT::datatable(d), 
                  widths = 5)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Interactive boxplot

The plotThe code











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~RACE,
  y = ~MATHS,
  color = ~RACE,
  type = "box"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Interactive scatterplot with tooltip

The plotThe code











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~MATHS,
  y = ~ENGLISH,
  color = ~RACE,
  text = ~paste(
    "ID:", ID,
    "<br>Class:", CLASS,
    "<br>Maths:", MATHS,
    "<br>English:", ENGLISH
  ),
  hoverinfo = "text",
  type = "scatter",
  mode = "markers"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Interactive violin plot

The plotThe code











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~RACE,
  y = ~MATHS,
  color = ~RACE,
  type = "violin",
  box = list(visible = TRUE),
  meanline = list(visible = TRUE)
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Interactive histogram

The plotThe code











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~MATHS,
  color = ~RACE,
  type = "histogram",
  opacity = 0.7
) %>%
  layout(
    barmode = "overlay",
    title = "Interactive Histogram of Maths Scores"
  )

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Interactive density heatmap

The plotThe code











plot_ly(
  data = exam_data,
  x = ~MATHS,
  y = ~ENGLISH,
  type = "histogram2d",
  colorscale = "YlOrRd"
)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Displaying standard deviation

The plotThe code











mean_sd <- function(x) {
  m <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  s <- sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  data.frame(
    y = m,
    ymin = m - s,
    ymax = m + s
  )
}

tooltip <- function(y, ymin, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean_val <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sd_val <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean_val, "\nSD:", sd_val)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE)) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS,
        tooltip = after_stat(
          tooltip(y, ymin, ymax)
        )),
    fun.data = mean_sd,                 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = "lightblue"
  ) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_sd,                 
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    size = 0.2
  )

  ggobj = gg_point,

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Animated line chart

The plotThe code














ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Year, y = Population, colour = Country)) +
  geom_line(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Population Growth Over Time: {frame_along}") +
  transition_reveal(Year)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Animated bar chart

The plotThe code














globalPop_continent <- globalPop %>%
  group_by(Year, Continent) %>%
  summarise(Population = sum(Population), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(globalPop_continent,
       aes(x = Continent,
           y = Population,
           fill = Continent)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Year: {frame_time}",
       x = "Continent",
       y = "Population") +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Animated area chart

The plotThe code














globalPop %>%
  group_by(Year, Continent) %>%
  summarise(Population = sum(Population), .groups = "drop") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = Year, 
             y = Population, 
             fill = Continent,
             group = Continent)) +
  geom_area(stat = "identity", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Global Population by Continent: {frame_along}") +
  transition_reveal(along = Year)

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Animated histogram

The plotThe code














ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Ageing Population: {frame_time}",
    x = "% Aged",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Animated scatter

The plotThe code














ggplot(globalPop,
       aes(x = Old,
           y = Young,
           colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~Continent) +
  labs(
    title = "Age Structure by Continent: {frame_time}",
    x = "% Aged",
    y = "% Young"
  ) +
  transition_time(Year) +
  ease_aes("linear")
